title: Diversification — what it actually does
subtitle: Diversification is not about owning more things. It is about owning things that do not move together. The distinction changes how you build a portfolio.

body.content: Owning 50 technology stocks is not diversification. Owning stocks, bonds, real estate, and international equities might be. The defining variable is not quantity — it is correlation. Assets that move together in the same direction provide no protection when the direction is down.

body.label: The mechanics
body.content: When two assets are perfectly correlated (correlation = 1), they move identically. Owning both adds no risk reduction. When two assets are uncorrelated (correlation = 0) or negatively correlated (correlation = -1), combining them reduces the portfolio's overall volatility without necessarily reducing expected return. This is the only free lunch in finance.
What diversification does and does not eliminate

items: Eliminates: idiosyncratic risk — the risk specific to a single company, sector, or geography.
Reduces: overall portfolio volatility when assets are imperfectly correlated.
Cannot eliminate: systematic risk — the broad market risk that affects all assets simultaneously (recessions, rate shocks, global crises).

body.content: The practical limit of diversification is important to understand. Adding a 51st stock to a 50-stock portfolio reduces risk by a negligible amount. The largest diversification gains come from the first 15-20 positions and from adding asset classes, not from adding more securities within the same class.

Diversification reduces the risk you should not be taking — company-specific and sector-specific risk. It does not eliminate market risk. And it only works when the assets you hold are genuinely uncorrelated.
